---
title: "Representation and performance: when ELL pays off"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
ELL turns a sparse mesh operator into a **dense rectangular loop**: two `(n, k_max)`
arrays, every row the same width, the gather-and-reduce XLA loves. That trade is a
win when rows are *uniform-length* — the icosphere, where every valence is 5 or 6 —
and a waste when they are not, because **every row pays for the worst row's
padding**. This page is the decision guide:

- **Dense vs ELL vs SectionedELL.** The cost model is $O(n\cdot k_{\max})$; ELL pays
  off when $k_{\max}$ is close to the typical degree, and falls off the *padding
  cliff* when a few high-valence vertices inflate $k_{\max}$.
- **The cliff's cure** is `SectionedELL`: bucket rows by degree, pad each bucket to
  its own small $k_{\max}$, so memory tracks the *largest bucket*, not the worst row.
- **Why this whole tier is XLA, not Pallas.** The ELL matvec needs a per-row gather,
  which the pinned JAX's Triton backend cannot lower — so `backend='jax'` is the GA
  path, and `'pallas-cuda'`/`'auto'` resolve then fall back with one warning.

The sibling story in volumes is the **flat-box fast path** (`lax.reduce_window`, no
patch tensor) versus the **im2col path** for explicit structuring elements, whose
memory scales with the window's *volume*.
:::

This is a **reference-tier** page. The narrative chapters
([operators](operators.qmd), [Laplace–Beltrami](laplace-beltrami.qmd),
[volumes](volumes.qmd)) teach *what* the operators compute and *why* they converge
to the smooth truth; this page is about *how they are stored and dispatched* so the
computation is fast and fits in memory. It is more engineering-flavoured than its
siblings, but the same posture holds throughout: a discrete mesh or grid is a
**finite approximation** of a smooth object, and we want the representation of that
approximation to scale with the geometry's real structure — near-uniform valence,
short voxel windows — rather than its worst case. It assumes the
[foundations](foundations.qmd) page's **execution classes**
([DIFF-JAX / HOST-CTOR / HOST-QA](foundations.qmd#sec-classes)) and the
[ELL matvec](operators.qmd#sec-ell) from the operator layer.

## A motivating decision {#sec-motivation}

You have two surfaces on your desk. One is an **fsaverage icosphere** — a cortical
template at FreeSurfer's `ico6`/`ico7` resolution, built by recursive subdivision of
an icosahedron. Its valences are *boringly uniform*: every one of the original 12
vertices has degree 5, every vertex added by subdivision has degree 6, so
$\min\deg = 5$, $\max\deg = 6$, full stop (this is the topological signature the mesh
tests assert — see the [sparse-specialisations design note][spec-doc]). The other is
a **subject-native white-matter surface reconstructed by marching cubes**: mostly
well-meshed, but with a scatter of high-valence vertices where the isosurface
extractor stitched together an awkward neighbourhood, so its degree distribution has
a *long tail*.

Both surfaces support the same operation — say, smoothing a thickness map along the
surface with the [cotangent Laplacian](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent). The
question this page answers: **should the operator for each surface be stored as a
flat `ELL`, or as a `SectionedELL`?** And if you write `format='auto'`, what does
`nitrix` decide, and why? The answer falls out of one cost model.

[spec-doc]: https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/sparse-specialisations.md

## When ELL pays off {#sec-storage}

### The cost model in one line

Recall the [ELL layout](operators.qmd#sec-ell): an operator is two dense arrays,
`values` and `indices`, each of shape $(n, k_{\max})$, where row $i$ holds the
weights and column-indices of vertex $i$'s neighbours, and rows shorter than
$k_{\max}$ are **padded** with a semiring-identity value (a no-op) at an in-range
sentinel index. The matvec is a fixed double loop: for each row, for each of the
$k_{\max}$ slots, gather one entry of the operand and combine it into the reduction.

So both the storage and the compute are exactly

$$
\text{cost} \;=\; O(n \cdot k_{\max}),
$$ {#eq-ell-cost}

where $n$ is the number of rows (vertices) and $k_{\max}$ is the padded row width.
There is no per-row branching, no ragged dimension — that uniformity is *the entire
reason* ELL is fast on XLA. But it is also where the trap is: $k_{\max}$ is set by
the **single longest row**, and every other row pays for it. The useful work is only
$\sum_i \deg_i$ (the real non-pad entries); everything beyond that is padding the
hardware still streams through.

### Where the work actually goes: the waste fraction

Put a number on the waste. Of the $n\cdot k_{\max}$ slots ELL stores and visits,
only $\sum_i \deg_i$ carry signal; the rest are pad. So the **wasted fraction** is

$$
\text{waste} \;=\; \frac{n\,k_{\max} - \sum_i \deg_i}{n\,k_{\max}}
            \;=\; 1 - \frac{\bar{\deg}}{k_{\max}},
$$ {#eq-waste}

where $\bar{\deg} = \tfrac1n\sum_i \deg_i$ is the mean degree. Read off the two
limits. When every row is the same length, $k_{\max} = \bar{\deg}$ and the waste is
**zero** — ELL is a perfect fit. When one hub vertex pushes $k_{\max}$ far above the
mean, $\bar{\deg}/k_{\max}\to 0$ and the waste approaches **100%** — you are storing
and streaming a mostly-empty rectangle.

This is exactly the difference between the two surfaces from
[the motivating decision](#sec-motivation). @fig-perf-valence shows it: the
icosphere's degrees pile up at 5 and 6, so $k_{\max}=6\approx\bar{\deg}$ and the
waste is small. The irregular mesh has the same *typical* degree but a long tail, so
its $k_{\max}$ is several times the mean and most of the flat-ELL rectangle is pad.

```{python}
#| label: fig-perf-valence
#| fig-cap: "Flat-ELL padding waste. Left: an icosphere (built here by hand from an icosahedron + 3 subdivisions) — valences cluster at 5–6, so k_max ≈ mean degree and almost nothing is wasted. Right: a synthetic irregular mesh with the same typical degree but a long valence tail — k_max is many times the mean, so the flat ELL is mostly pad. Annotated: stored slots n·k_max, used (non-pad) entries Σdeg, and the wasted slots between them."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)


def icosahedron():
    phi = (1.0 + np.sqrt(5.0)) / 2.0
    verts = np.array([
        [-1,  phi, 0], [1,  phi, 0], [-1, -phi, 0], [1, -phi, 0],
        [0, -1,  phi], [0, 1,  phi], [0, -1, -phi], [0, 1, -phi],
        [phi, 0, -1], [phi, 0, 1], [-phi, 0, -1], [-phi, 0, 1],
    ], dtype=float)
    verts /= np.linalg.norm(verts, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    faces = np.array([
        [0, 11, 5], [0, 5, 1], [0, 1, 7], [0, 7, 10], [0, 10, 11],
        [1, 5, 9], [5, 11, 4], [11, 10, 2], [10, 7, 6], [7, 1, 8],
        [3, 9, 4], [3, 4, 2], [3, 2, 6], [3, 6, 8], [3, 8, 9],
        [4, 9, 5], [2, 4, 11], [6, 2, 10], [8, 6, 7], [9, 8, 1],
    ], dtype=int)
    return verts, faces


def subdivide(verts, faces):
    verts = list(map(tuple, verts))
    index = {v: i for i, v in enumerate(verts)}
    cache = {}

    def midpoint(a, b):
        key = (min(a, b), max(a, b))
        if key in cache:
            return cache[key]
        m = (np.array(verts[a]) + np.array(verts[b])) / 2.0
        m = m / np.linalg.norm(m)
        mt = tuple(m)
        idx = index.get(mt)
        if idx is None:
            idx = len(verts)
            verts.append(mt)
            index[mt] = idx
        cache[key] = idx
        return idx

    new_faces = []
    for a, b, c in faces:
        ab, bc, ca = midpoint(a, b), midpoint(b, c), midpoint(c, a)
        new_faces += [[a, ab, ca], [b, bc, ab], [c, ca, bc], [ab, bc, ca]]
    return np.array(verts), np.array(new_faces)


def valences(n, faces):
    nbr = [set() for _ in range(n)]
    for a, b, c in faces:
        for u, v in ((a, b), (b, c), (c, a)):
            nbr[u].add(v); nbr[v].add(u)
    return np.array([len(s) for s in nbr])


v, f = icosahedron()
for _ in range(3):
    v, f = subdivide(v, f)
ico_deg = valences(len(v), f)

# A synthetic irregular mesh: well-meshed core + a long high-valence tail.
n_irr = ico_deg.size
irr_deg = np.clip(rng.integers(5, 8, n_irr)
                  + (rng.pareto(2.0, n_irr) * 6).astype(int), 3, 60)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4))
bins = np.arange(2, 62)
axes[0].hist(ico_deg, bins=bins, color="#4477aa")
axes[0].set_title(f"Icosphere (n={ico_deg.size}): valence 5–6")
axes[0].set_xlabel("vertex degree"); axes[0].set_ylabel("count")
axes[0].set_xlim(2, 30)
axes[1].hist(irr_deg, bins=bins, color="#cc6677")
axes[1].axvline(irr_deg.max(), color="k", ls="--", lw=1)
axes[1].set_title(f"Irregular mesh: long tail, k_max={irr_deg.max()}")
axes[1].set_xlabel("vertex degree"); axes[1].set_xlim(2, 62)

for ax, deg in zip(axes, (ico_deg, irr_deg)):
    n, kmax = deg.size, int(deg.max())
    stored, used = n * kmax, int(deg.sum())
    waste = stored - used
    ax.text(0.97, 0.80,
            f"n·k_max = {stored:,}\nused = {used:,}\n"
            f"wasted = {waste:,} ({waste / stored:.0%})",
            transform=ax.transAxes, ha="right", va="top", fontsize=9,
            bbox=dict(boxstyle="round", fc="white", ec="0.7"))

fig.suptitle("Flat-ELL padding waste: near-uniform vs long-tailed valence")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Dense, ELL, or sectioned — the three regimes

With @eq-ell-cost and @eq-waste in hand the decision is mechanical.

- **Dense $n\times n$.** Costs $O(n^2)$ storage and compute regardless of structure.
  Only competitive when the operator is *tiny* (so $n^2$ is small) or *genuinely
  dense* (so $k_{\max}\approx n$ and ELL saves nothing). For a cortical mesh with
  $n\approx 3\times10^5$ and $\deg\approx 6$ it is hopeless — hundreds of gigabytes
  of mostly zeros, as the [operators page](operators.qmd#sec-neighbours) spells out.
- **Flat ELL.** Costs $O(n\,k_{\max})$ with the waste of @eq-waste. The right choice
  when $k_{\max}\approx\bar{\deg}$: the icosphere, the fsaverage family, regular
  voxel-grid stencils (where every voxel uses the *same* offset pattern, so the row
  width is fixed by construction). This is the default, `format='ell'`.
- **SectionedELL.** Costs the sum of per-bucket $O(n_b\,k_{\max,b})$ — pays the cliff
  off when the degree spread is wide. The next section.

The headline: **ELL pays off precisely when the geometry's valence is near-uniform.**
That is not an accident of the format — it is the format *designed for* the meshes
neuroimaging actually uses most (templated spheres), with `SectionedELL` as the
escape hatch for the irregular case.

## The padding cliff and its cure: SectionedELL {#sec-padding}

### The idea: bucket rows by degree

The flat ELL's flaw is that one long row sets the width for all $n$ rows. The cure
is the simplest thing that could possibly work: **group rows of similar degree, and
pad each group only to its own modest $k_{\max}$.** A row of degree 6 then sits in a
bucket whose width is ~8, not a global width of 60 dragged up by some degree-58 hub.

This is `SectionedELL` (`sparse/ell_sectioned.py`). It is, deliberately, a
*Python-level wrapper* around the existing [ELL matvec](operators.qmd#sec-ell) — **no
new kernel**. Construction partitions the rows into buckets, builds one ordinary
`ELL` per bucket at the bucket's local width, runs the kernel once per bucket, and
scatters the results back into the original row order.

The default bucketing function (`_default_bucket`) is

$$
b(\deg) \;=\; \big\lceil \log_2 \max(\deg, 1)\big\rceil,
$$ {#eq-bucket}

with each bucket's aligned width set to $k_{\max,b} = 2^{b}$ (and $k_{\max,0}=1$).
So degree 1 lands in bucket 0 (width 1); degree 2 in bucket 1 (width 2); degrees 3–4
in bucket 2 (width 4); degrees 5–8 in bucket 3 (width 8); and so on. The log scale
is the point: it keeps the *number* of buckets small ($\sim\log_2 k_{\max}$) while
guaranteeing that no row is padded to more than **twice** its own degree. Why align
each bucket to the next power of two rather than to its largest member? Because then a
`SectionedELL` reused across many calls — the common case, since the operator is
built once and applied repeatedly — compiles each bucket's matvec **once**: the
shapes are stable across calls, so XLA's trace cache hits.

### The memory win

Now compare storage. Flat ELL stores $n\cdot k_{\max}$ slots. SectionedELL stores

$$
\text{slots}_{\text{sectioned}} \;=\; \sum_b n_b\, k_{\max,b},
$$ {#eq-sectioned-cost}

where $n_b$ is the number of rows in bucket $b$. The decisive fact is that this sum
is dominated by the **largest bucket**, not by the worst-case row. On a long-tailed
distribution — distance-thresholded neighbourhoods in irregular point clouds, where
$k_{\max}$ can be 10–100× the median, or atlas-parcel adjacencies whose parcel sizes
span one to two orders of magnitude (both noted in the
[`ell_sectioned` module docstring][ellsec-src]) — the handful of hub rows live alone
in a high-numbered bucket and pay their own width, while the millions of typical rows
pay only ~8. @fig-perf-sectioned shows the total stored slots collapse by roughly an
order of magnitude on such a distribution.

`SectionedELL` exposes this directly: `.total_storage` returns
$\sum_b n_b\,k_{\max,b}$ so you can verify the win against a flat ELL's
$n\cdot\max_b k_{\max,b}$, and `.n_buckets` reports how many sections were emitted
(empty buckets are skipped).

[ellsec-src]: https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell_sectioned.py

```{python}
#| label: fig-perf-sectioned
#| fig-cap: "The memory win, on a long-tailed valence distribution. Left: total stored per-row slots — flat ELL (n·k_max) vs SectionedELL (Σ_b n_b·k_max,b, the real ceil(log2 deg) bucketing with 2^b aligned widths) vs the irreducible 'used' floor (Σ deg). Right: where the sectioned storage lives — it is dominated by the largest populated bucket, not by the one hub row that sets the flat k_max."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# A long-tailed valence distribution: small median, worst case 10-100x larger.
n = 20000
deg = np.clip(rng.integers(5, 8, n) + (rng.pareto(1.5, n) * 8).astype(int), 1, None)
median, kmax = max(int(np.median(deg)), 1), int(deg.max())


def bucket(d):
    return 0 if d <= 1 else int(d - 1).bit_length()


def bucket_kmax(b):
    return 1 if b == 0 else 2 ** b


buckets = {}
for d in deg:
    buckets.setdefault(bucket(d), []).append(int(d))

flat_slots = n * kmax
sec_per_bucket = {b: len(ds) * bucket_kmax(b) for b, ds in buckets.items()}
sec_slots = sum(sec_per_bucket.values())
used = int(deg.sum())

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.2))
labels = ["flat ELL\n(n·k_max)", "SectionedELL\n(Σ buckets)", "used\n(non-pad)"]
vals = [flat_slots, sec_slots, used]
bars = axes[0].bar(labels, vals, color=["#cc6677", "#4477aa", "#999999"])
axes[0].set_ylabel("stored per-row slots")
axes[0].set_title(f"median deg={median}, k_max={kmax}: "
                  f"{flat_slots / sec_slots:.1f}× memory win")
for b, val in zip(bars, vals):
    axes[0].text(b.get_x() + b.get_width() / 2, val, f"{val:,}",
                 ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=9)
axes[0].set_ylim(0, flat_slots * 1.12)

bs = sorted(sec_per_bucket)
axes[1].bar([f"b{b}\nk={bucket_kmax(b)}" for b in bs],
            [sec_per_bucket[b] for b in bs], color="#4477aa")
axes[1].set_title("SectionedELL slots per bucket")
axes[1].set_xlabel("bucket (degree range)"); axes[1].set_ylabel("stored slots")

fig.suptitle("The padding cliff: flat ELL vs degree-bucketed SectionedELL")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### The real surface: `format={'ell','sectioned','auto'}`

You rarely call `sectioned_ell_from_ragged` by hand. The mesh constructors —
`mesh_k_ring_adjacency` and `mesh_cotangent_laplacian` in `sparse/mesh.py` — take a
`format` argument with three settings:

- **`format='ell'`** (the default) — a flat `ELL` padded to the global max degree,
  byte-identical to the format's original behaviour.
- **`format='sectioned'`** — always a degree-bucketed `SectionedELL`.
- **`format='auto'`** — section *only when it is worth it.*

The `'auto'` rule is the one to internalise, because it operationalises this whole
page. It is in `_resolve_format`: compute the **ratio of the max degree to the median
degree**, and section when that ratio exceeds a threshold:

$$
\text{section} \iff \frac{\max_i \deg_i}{\operatorname{median}_i \deg_i} > 2.
$$ {#eq-auto}

The threshold is `_AUTO_SECTION_RATIO = 2.0`. Why max-over-median rather than, say,
max-over-mean? The median is robust to exactly the long tail we care about: a handful
of degree-58 hubs barely move the median but blow up the mean, so a max/median ratio
cleanly separates "near-uniform" from "long-tailed." For the icosphere /
fsaverage families the ratio is $6/6=1$ — comfortably under 2 — so `'auto'` keeps
them flat; marching-cubes output with a high-valence tail trips the threshold and
sections. (This is the same surface pairing from
[the motivating decision](#sec-motivation), now decided automatically.)

One detail worth stressing because it is *not* guessed: the design's bucket
boundaries and the choice of threshold are calibrated against the **measured**
valence histograms of real cortical surfaces, not chosen by intuition — the
[`ell_sectioned` rationale][ellsec-src] points specifically at distance-thresholded
point-cloud neighbourhoods and atlas-parcel adjacencies as the motivating
distributions. The cost model decides; the data sets the constants.

### The public surface of the sectioned tier

For completeness, the format's full public API (`__all__` in the two modules), tagged
by execution class:

- **`ELL`** — the dataclass: `values`, `indices`, `n_cols`, `identity`, with
  `n_rows`/`k_max`/`shape`/`dtype` properties. Registered as a JAX pytree, so an
  `ELL` flows through `jit`/`vmap`/`grad` as a first-class operand. **DIFF-JAX** as an
  operand (the `values` are a differentiable leaf; `indices` is integer and carries
  an inert `float0` cotangent).
- **`ell_pad`** (**HOST-CTOR**) — pad ragged per-row lists to a fixed `k_max`,
  filling `values` with `identity` and `indices` with an in-range `pad_index`.
- **`ell_from_dense`** (**HOST-CTOR**) — convert a dense matrix to ELL by keeping the
  top-`k_max`-by-magnitude entries per row. Note its documented hazard: top-$k$
  selection is **not symmetry-preserving**, so the stored pattern is generally
  asymmetric even when the input is symmetric; the remedies are `symmetrize=True`
  (which can densify hub rows entirely — its storage is *not* bounded by a small
  multiple of `k_max`, exactly the cliff this page is about) or the storage-free
  matvec symmetrisation $\tfrac12(Ax + A^\top x)$ on the spectral consumers.
- **`ell_mask`** (**DIFF-JAX**) — mask edges out by rewriting their value to the
  algebra's **annihilator** (prefer `semiring=`; the `identity=` form is deprecated).
  The medial-wall / grey-matter case: edges into a masked region contribute the
  identity to every reduction. `EUCLIDEAN` has no annihilator, so masking by value is
  impossible there — drop the columns instead.
- **`ell_add_self_loops`** (**DIFF-JAX**) — append an $(i,i)$ edge per row (the GCN
  $\hat A = A + I$ trick / attention's self-attention), growing `k_max` by one;
  optional per-edge attribute fill `'mean'`/`'add'`/`'zero'`.
- **`ell_to_dense`** (**DIFF-JAX**) — scatter back to a dense matrix, for tests and
  fallback paths.
- **`SectionedELL`** — the bucketed dataclass: `sections` (one `ELL` per bucket),
  `row_groups` (the original-row indices per bucket, stored as host `np.ndarray` so
  the dispatch loop can use them as static scatter indices), plus `total_storage` and
  `n_buckets`. Also a JAX pytree; the *number* of buckets is structural (encoded in
  the treedef) so the static Python dispatch length survives a trace.
- **`sectioned_ell_from_ragged`** (**HOST-CTOR**) — build a `SectionedELL` from ragged
  per-row lists; accepts a custom `bucket_by`. Construction is host-side and *not*
  JIT-compatible (the per-bucket shapes are data-dependent).
- **`sectioned_semiring_ell_matmul`** (**DIFF-JAX**) — the forward matvec: per-bucket
  `semiring_ell_matmul` then scatter-set back to original row order. Empty (degree-0)
  rows live in bucket 0 with all-identity values, so their output is the algebra
  identity — the algebra-correct "no neighbours, no contribution."
- **`sectioned_semiring_ell_rmatvec`** (**DIFF-JAX**) — the adjoint $Y = A^\top X$,
  **REAL only** (the additive scatter adjoint); composing forward + adjoint gives the
  symmetric-part matvec for spectral solvers on bucketed adjacencies whose
  construction did not preserve symmetry.

The format-agnostic seam over all of this is [`apply_operator`](operators.qmd#sec-apply)
(`sparse/_apply.py`): it runs `op @ x` whether `op` is a flat `ELL` or a
`SectionedELL`, vmapping leading batch axes over the 2-D core, so your algorithm code
never branches on the storage choice.

## XLA, not Pallas {#sec-backend}

### Why there is no custom kernel for the ELL matvec

A reader who has met Triton might ask: this gather-and-reduce is a textbook GPU
kernel — why not write a fused Pallas/Triton version and skip XLA's overhead? The
[`ell-on-triton` decision record][triton-doc] answers this, and the answer is
**structural, not a tuning regret.**

Look at what one step of the ELL K-loop needs. For a tile of output rows and columns,
on every step $p$ it reads `values[i, p]` (contiguous — fine) and
`B[indices[i, p], j]` — a **per-row gather of the dense operand**, a different row
index per output row $i$. That second access lowers, in JAX, to `lax.gather`. And the
pinned JAX's **Pallas Triton backend cannot lower `gather`**: it raises
`NotImplementedError: Unimplemented primitive in Pallas GPU lowering: gather`. The
record documents three attempted workarounds — advanced indexing `B_blk[idx_p]`,
`jnp.take(..., axis=0)`, and Python-unrolled per-row loads with an axis-0
`jnp.stack` — and all three hit the same wall (the last because Triton lowers
`concatenate` only along the *last* axis). So the format whose whole purpose is the
per-row gather cannot, today, be expressed in this Pallas backend.

[triton-doc]: https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/ell-on-triton.md

### The policy: resolve, fall back, warn, re-probe

The consequence is a clean, honest dispatch policy (the same `backend` knob threads
through `semiring_ell_matmul`, `sectioned_semiring_ell_matmul`, and `apply_operator`):

- **`backend='jax'`** — works. This is the GA path. The reference walks the K-dim
  with `lax.fori_loop` and `B[idx_p]`; it is correct, **differentiable** (the
  backward composes the per-algebra rule with `scatter_add` for the operand
  gradient), and reasonably fast on Ampere.
- **`backend='pallas-cuda'`** — resolved by the dispatcher, then *immediately falls
  back* to JAX with one `NitrixBackendFallback` warning per `(shape, dtype, algebra)`
  (deduplicated so a vmapped batch warns once, not once per element).
- **`backend='auto'`** — on Ampere+ resolves to `pallas-cuda`, then falls back, same
  as above. This is *intended*: the warning surfaces the policy rather than silently
  doing the slow-or-fast thing.

A `confirm_pallas_falls_back()` probe in the G0 bench re-checks the structural
fallback on each run; if a future JAX release lands `gather` lowering, the probe
flips and the team knows to swap the stub for a real kernel. The public dispatcher
does not change either way.

For a sense of scale, the frozen G0 baseline (A10G, fp32, mesh-adjacency
$m=4096$, $k_{\max}=32$, $n_{\text{col}}=32$) records, on the JAX path: REAL
$\approx 346\,\mu s$, LOG $\approx 466\,\mu s$, max-plus $\approx 356\,\mu s$ — the
numbers downstream consumers should plan around (see the [decision record][triton-doc]
for the full table).

### The morphology counterpart: the im2col cliff

The volume operators have the same shape of story — a fast fused path and a
materialising slow path — but the trigger is the *structuring element*, not the
valence. From `morphology/_mm.py` (and the measured [B21 cost FR][b21]):

- **Flat box** (`structuring_element=None`) → `_windowed_reduce`, a single fused
  `lax.reduce_window`. Dilation becomes a sliding-window max, erosion a sliding-window
  min; XLA lowers it like a pooling op. **No patch tensor**, HBM tracks the image,
  and it *beats cupy* (~0.56–0.76× on the L4 box cases). This is the common case and
  the page-representative point.
- **Explicit structuring element** (a disk, ball, cross, ring) →
  `_conv_wrap → semiring_conv`, the **im2col path**: it gathers a
  `(*spatial, prod(window))` **patch tensor** before the tropical matmul. That tensor
  scales with the window's *volume*, and it is the memory blow-up: a 64³ ball of
  radius 2 peaks at **~336 MB** of HBM and runs **~9–11× slower** than cupy (versus
  cupy's ~0.26–1.05 MB across the board). The disk/ball footprint is the *default* in
  skimage and the common scipy choice, so this is the path real users hit.

@fig-perf-morphology plots both: the measured peak-HBM bars (fused vs im2col) and the
cost law — the im2col patch tensor is $\text{image voxels}\times\text{window volume}$
elements, so it climbs steeply with the SE edge length while the fused path stays
flat at roughly the image size.

[b21]: https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/feature-requests/morphology-explicit-se-im2col-cost.md

```{python}
#| label: fig-perf-morphology
#| fig-cap: "Morphology dispatch. Left: measured peak HBM (L4, fp32) from the B21 cost characterisation — the flat-box reduce_window path (blue) stays near the image size; every explicit disk/ball SE (red) materialises an im2col patch tensor whose footprint runs to hundreds of MB. Right: the cost law — the patch tensor is (image voxels × window volume), so its HBM climbs with the SE edge for 2-D and 3-D grids while the fused path is window-volume-independent."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Patch

# Measured peak HBM (L4, fp32) from morphology-explicit-se-im2col-cost.md.
cases = [
    ("box 3\n256²", 0.79, "reduce_window"),
    ("box 3\n64³", 3.15, "reduce_window"),
    ("disk r=3\n256²", 93.0, "im2col"),
    ("disk r=7\n256²", 193.0, "im2col"),
    ("ball r=2\n64³", 336.0, "im2col"),
]
names = [c[0] for c in cases]
hbm = [c[1] for c in cases]
colors = ["#4477aa" if c[2] == "reduce_window" else "#cc6677" for c in cases]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.2))
bars = axes[0].bar(names, hbm, color=colors, log=True)
axes[0].set_ylabel("peak HBM high-water (MB, log)")
axes[0].set_title("Measured peak memory (L4, fp32)")
for b, h in zip(bars, hbm):
    axes[0].text(b.get_x() + b.get_width() / 2, h, f"{h:g}",
                 ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=8)
axes[0].legend(handles=[
    Patch(color="#4477aa", label="flat box → reduce_window (fused)"),
    Patch(color="#cc6677", label="explicit SE → im2col patches"),
], fontsize=8, loc="upper left")

edges = np.arange(1, 16)
img2d, img3d, bpe = 256 ** 2, 64 ** 3, 4
axes[1].plot(edges, img2d * edges ** 2 * bpe / 1e6, "o-", color="#cc6677",
             label="2-D im2col (256² grid)")
axes[1].plot(edges, img3d * edges ** 3 * bpe / 1e6, "s-", color="#882255",
             label="3-D im2col (64³ grid)")
axes[1].axhline(img3d * bpe / 1e6, color="#4477aa", ls="--",
                label="reduce_window HBM ≈ image (flat)")
axes[1].set_yscale("log")
axes[1].set_xlabel("structuring-element edge length")
axes[1].set_ylabel("patch-tensor size (MB, log)")
axes[1].set_title("im2col HBM scales with window VOLUME")
axes[1].legend(fontsize=8)

fig.suptitle("Morphology dispatch: the flat-box fast path vs the im2col cliff")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### A subtlety: `jit(grad)` and the concrete identity

There is a numerical-engineering trap in the fast path worth knowing, because it
explains a curious line in `_windowed_reduce`. `lax.reduce_window` only routes a
generic reducer (`lax.max` / `lax.min`) to its **differentiable specialised
primitive** (`reduce_window_max_p` / `reduce_window_min_p`) when JAX's monoid
detection sees a *concrete* identity equal to the dtype's max/min identity. If the
window-init scalar is a *traced* value — which `jnp.asarray(...)` becomes under
`jit` — detection fails, falls back to the generic `reduce_window_p`, and that
primitive's **missing transpose rule breaks `jit(grad(...))`**. The fix is to pass
the init as a NumPy-concrete scalar (`np.asarray(identity, x.dtype)`, *not*
`jnp.asarray`). The lesson generalises: on this tier, *how* you construct a constant
can decide whether the op is differentiable — concrete identities route to the
diff-friendly primitive.

## Execution-class cost implications {#sec-classes-cost}

Everything above ties back to the [execution-class taxonomy](foundations.qmd#sec-classes).
The governing rule is **never call a HOST op inside a jitted loop** — and the
performance reason is now concrete.

The expensive *construction* steps are all **HOST-CTOR**: sectioning a ragged
adjacency (`sectioned_ell_from_ragged`, data-dependent per-bucket shapes), building a
mesh's k-ring or cotangent operator (host-side BFS over faces), [marching
cubes](volume-surface.qmd#sec-marching), and the spatial **broad-phase index** for
point-to-triangle queries. These are designed to be **amortised**: built *once* per
mesh or per atlas, then reused across every subsequent matvec — and across a vmapped
batch of images that share the topology. The per-iteration work that *does* run
inside `jit`/`vmap`/`grad` stays **DIFF-JAX**: the ELL/SectionedELL matvec, `ell_mask`,
`apply_operator`. Get this boundary wrong — call a HOST-CTOR like
`sectioned_ell_from_ragged` *inside* a jitted training loop — and you re-pay the
host-side construction on every iteration (and likely retrace, since its output
shapes are data-dependent), turning a build-once cost into a per-step cost. That is
the single most expensive mistake this tier invites.

Two scale crossovers the feature-requests quantify, so you can choose the right tool
rather than discovering the wall in production:

- **Connected components.** The pointer-jumping label propagation
  (`morphology/_label.py`: a `lax.while_loop` of neighbour-max hops + pointer jumps,
  $O(\log d)$ passes for diameter $d$) is correct and jittable, but its per-pass cost
  is $O(N)$ over the whole label image and it does **not** amortise the way
  `cupyx.scipy.ndimage.label` does: nitrix's `connected_components` grows from
  ~1.30 ms at $48^3$ to **~15.7 ms at $160^3$** while cupyx stays roughly flat
  (~0.6–0.85 ms), a gap widening from ~2× to **~18×** ([morphology GPU-scaling
  FR][gpuscale]). At brain scale this is a known kernel/algorithm candidate, not a
  free lunch.
- **Euclidean distance transform.** `distance_transform_edt` is the separable
  **min-plus semiring** EDT — it searches *all* parabolas rather than the
  Felzenszwalb–Huttenlocher lower envelope. That is shallow and high-FLOP, so it
  **wins at small scale and loses at large scale** on a parallel GPU: ~2.4× ahead of
  cupyx at $48^3$, roughly par through $128^3$, ~2.2× behind by $160^3$, with the
  crossover near $96^3$ ([same FR][gpuscale]). This is the deliberate semiring
  trade-off, not a regression — and exactly the kind of *class/correlation*
  reasoning (where does this discrete method's cost curve cross the reference's?)
  this unit favours over chasing a single headline number.

[gpuscale]: https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/feature-requests/morphology-gpu-scaling.md

The broad-phase spatial index for nearest-triangle / point-location queries
([mesh-spatial-acceleration][meshacc]) is the cleanest illustration of the amortise
discipline *and* of this unit's validation posture. It replaces a brute-force
$O(n_{\text{query}}\cdot n_{\text{faces}})$ scan with a uniform-grid candidate prune,
host-side and **HOST-CTOR** (dynamic ragged candidate gathering does not lower
cleanly to XLA — the mesh tier's host-construct-then-array pattern again). Its
correctness is not asserted by a tolerance: the accelerated path is validated by
**bit-exact parity** (`np.array_equal`) against the brute-force reference on the
icosphere, an analytic sphere SDF, and real fsaverage white/pial pairs, with an
unconditional brute-force fallback for any query the index cannot resolve. This is
the unit's two-pronged validation in one place — an **analytic oracle** (the sphere
SDF matches $\lVert x\rVert - r$) for the *physics*, and parity-vs-reference for the
*acceleration* — never trading correctness for speed.

[meshacc]: https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/feature-requests/mesh-spatial-acceleration.md

## Further reading

- Bell, N. & Garland, M. (2009). *Implementing sparse matrix-vector multiplication
  on throughput-oriented processors.* Proceedings of the Conference on High
  Performance Computing Networking, Storage and Analysis (SC '09). — the origin of
  the ELL and hybrid (HYB) formats and the padding-cliff analysis this page rests on.
- Saad, Y. (2003). *Iterative Methods for Sparse Linear Systems* (2nd ed.). SIAM. —
  background on sparse storage formats (CSR, ELLPACK/ITPACK) and sparse matvec.
- Felzenszwalb, P. F. & Huttenlocher, D. P. (2012). *Distance transforms of sampled
  functions.* Theory of Computing, 8(19), 415–428. — the lower-envelope EDT the
  semiring brute-force trades against at large scale.
- The JAX documentation on `lax.gather` / `lax.scatter` and Pallas GPU lowering, and
  on `lax.reduce_window` and its differentiable specialisations — the primitives
  whose lowering behaviour sets this tier's backend policy.
- Pinkall, U. & Polthier, K. (1993). *Computing discrete minimal surfaces and their
  conjugates.* Experimental Mathematics, 2(1), 15–36. — the cotangent operator whose
  bucketed storage motivates `format='auto'`.

## See also

- Source — sparse formats:
  [`sparse/ell.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell.py),
  [`sparse/ell_sectioned.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/ell_sectioned.py),
  [`sparse/mesh.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)
  (the `format='auto'` dispatch),
  [`sparse/_apply.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/_apply.py).
- Source — kernels and morphology:
  [`semiring/ell.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/ell.py),
  [`morphology/_mm.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/morphology/_mm.py),
  [`morphology/_label.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/morphology/_label.py).
- Design / decision records:
  [`docs/design/ell-on-triton.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/ell-on-triton.md),
  [`docs/design/sparse-specialisations.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/sparse-specialisations.md),
  [`docs/feature-requests/morphology-explicit-se-im2col-cost.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/feature-requests/morphology-explicit-se-im2col-cost.md),
  [`docs/feature-requests/morphology-gpu-scaling.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/feature-requests/morphology-gpu-scaling.md),
  [`docs/feature-requests/mesh-spatial-acceleration.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/feature-requests/mesh-spatial-acceleration.md).
- Neighbouring pages: [the operator algebra](operators.qmd#sec-ell) (ELL and the
  semiring matvec this page sizes) · [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-classes)
  (execution classes) · [volumes](volumes.qmd#sec-morphology) (the morphology ops) ·
  [solvers](solvers.qmd) (the iterative consumers of these operators). Cross-unit:
  [HSGP](../statistics/hsgp.qmd#sec-hsgp) in the stats unit for a comparable
  storage-vs-accuracy trade in the spectral-prior setting.
